edges.description: Reduced COUP-TFI dosage perturbs its transcriptional control of cortical arealization, neuronal differentiation, and visual-pathway development.

nodes.description: COUP-TFI is a major transcriptional regulator orchestrating cortical arealization, cell-type specification, and maturation across forebrain structures, and contributes to development of the retina and optic nerve. Its haploinsufficiency disrupts these developmental programs, producing the combined neurodevelopmental and visual phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: The pleiotropic transcriptional regulator COUP-TFI plays multiple roles in neural development and disease.
evidence.snippet: COUP-TFI (or NR2F1), a nuclear receptor belonging to the superfamily of the steroid/thyroid hormone receptors, and considered to be one of the major transcriptional regulators orchestrating cortical arealization, cell-type specification and maturation.

evidence.reference_title: NR2F1 mutations cause optic atrophy with intellectual disability.
evidence.snippet: These findings indicate that NR2F1 plays an important role in the neurodevelopment of the visual system and that its disruption can lead to optic atrophy with intellectual disability.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic NR2F1 variants cause a developmental ocular phenotype recapitulated in a mutant mouse model.
evidence.snippet: Abnormal retinogenesis in early stages of development was observed in Nr2f1 mutant mice with decreased retinal ganglion cell density and disruption of retinal ganglion cell axonal guidance from the neural retina into the optic stalk, accounting for the development of optic nerve hypoplasia.

evidence.reference_title: Nr2f1 heterozygous knockout mice recapitulate neurological phenotypes of Bosch-Boonstra-Schaaf optic atrophy syndrome and show impaired hippocampal synaptic plasticity.
evidence.snippet: we investigated a heterozygous knockout mouse model and found that this model recapitulates some of the neurological phenotypes of BBSOAS, including altered learning/memory, hearing defects, neonatal hypotonia and decreased hippocampal volume.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic expansion of Bosch-Boonstra-Schaaf optic atrophy syndrome and further evidence for genotype-phenotype correlations.
evidence.snippet: Bosch-Boonstra-Schaaf Optic Atrophy Syndrome (BBSOAS) is an autosomal dominant neurodevelopmental disorder caused by loss-of-function variants in NR2F1

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic expansion of Bosch-Boonstra-Schaaf optic atrophy syndrome and further evidence for genotype-phenotype correlations.
evidence.snippet: we provide additional evidence for genotype-phenotype correlations, specifically supporting a more severe phenotype associated with DBD variants.

nodes.description: Heterozygous loss-of-function variants (missense, nonsense, frameshift, translation-initiation) and whole-gene deletions of NR2F1 reduce dosage of the COUP-TFI nuclear receptor transcription factor. Missense variants in the zinc-finger DNA-binding domain and the putative ligand-binding domain reduce NR2F1 transcriptional activity, and DNA-binding domain variants are associated with the most severe clinical phenotypes.

evidence.reference_title: NR2F1 mutations cause optic atrophy with intellectual disability.
evidence.snippet: NR2F1 encodes a nuclear receptor protein that regulates transcription. A reporter assay showed that missense mutations in the zinc-finger DNA-binding domain and the putative ligand-binding domain decrease NR2F1 transcriptional activity.

evidence.reference_title: The expanding clinical phenotype of Bosch-Boonstra-Schaaf optic atrophy syndrome: 20 new cases and possible genotype-phenotype correlations.
evidence.snippet: Bosch-Boonstra-Schaaf optic atrophy syndrome (BBSOAS) is an autosomal-dominant disorder characterized by optic atrophy and intellectual disability caused by loss-of-function mutations in NR2F1.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiological Heterogeneity of the BBSOA Neurodevelopmental Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: the Bosch-Boonstra-Schaaf Optic Atrophy Syndrome (BBSOAS; OMIM 615722; ORPHA 401777), a recently described monogenic neurodevelopmental syndrome caused by the haploinsufficiency of NR2F1 gene, a key transcriptional regulator of brain development.
